coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and smartphone-assisted visual detection of oxytetracycline by a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from banana peel cooperating with europium
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Chen
Zhou, Tong
Gao, Zhe
Li, Meijiao
Zhou, Qian
Zhao, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Oxytetracycline (OTC) residues have serious adverse effects on human health. Thus, selective and sensitive field detection methods are needed. Here, we successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with good water-solubility, prominent fluorescence (FL), and favorable stability via a one-step hydrothermal method using banana peel as a carbon source and diethylenetriamine as a nitrogen source. We used the N-CQDs to create a ratiometric FL probe by self-assembling them with europium ions (Eu3+), enabling OTC detection. The FL intensity of N-CQDs at 445 nm was quenched due to the inner filter effect, while the FL intensity of Eu3+ at 621 nm markedly increased via the antenna effect upon OTC introduction. The ratiometric FL sensor demonstrated two linear relationships with OTC concentrations, ranging from 0.01 to 5 μg mL−1 and 5 to 25 μg mL−1, with a limit-of-detection of 4.9 ng mL−1 for OTC. Additionally, this platform depicted an apparent color change from blue to purple under a 365 nm ultraviolet–visible lamp, and a visual semi-quantitative analysis of OTC was achieved using a mobile phone application. We successfully implemented this method to detect OTC in pork muscle and swine urine, achieving remarkable recovery results (96.89–103.20%). Therefore, our findings demonstrate the potential of this approach for real-time and semi-quantitative OTC detection.

(b) The linear relationships between F621/F445 and the concentration of OTC. (c) The emission spectra of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor with the addition of OTC.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and smartphone-assisted visual detection of oxytetracycline by a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from banana peel cooperating with europium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, Hebei, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, Hebei, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Lekai South Street 2596, Baoding 071001, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) residues have serious adverse effects on human health. Thus, selective and sensitive field detection methods are needed. Here, we successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with good water-solubility, prominent fluorescence (FL), and favorable stability via a one-step hydrothermal method using banana peel as a carbon source and diethylenetriamine as a nitrogen source. We used the N-CQDs to create a ratiometric FL probe by self-assembling them with europium ions (Eu3+), enabling OTC detection. The FL intensity of N-CQDs at 445 nm was quenched due to the inner filter effect, while the FL intensity of Eu3+ at 621 nm markedly increased via the antenna effect upon OTC introduction. The ratiometric FL sensor demonstrated two linear relationships with OTC concentrations, ranging from 0.01 to 5 μg mL−1 and 5 to 25 μg mL−1, with a limit-of-detection of 4.9 ng mL−1 for OTC. Additionally, this platform depicted an apparent color change from blue to purple under a 365 nm ultraviolet–visible lamp, and a visual semi-quantitative analysis of OTC was achieved using a mobile phone application. We successfully implemented this method to detect OTC in pork muscle and swine urine, achieving remarkable recovery results (96.89–103.20%). Therefore, our findings demonstrate the potential of this approach for real-time and semi-quantitative OTC detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a member of the versatile tetracycline class of antibiotics that is extensively employed in treating and preventing illnesses in animals and humans by interfering with protein synthesis [1,2]. Nevertheless, overdose or long-term low-dose OTC use can result in residues in food, water, the environment, and soils that are difficult to degrade and pose serious threats to human health, including liver damage, allergic reactions, and bacterial resistance [3,4].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were serendipitously discovered as a new class of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in 2004 [5] and officially named in 2006 [6]. CQDs have gained increasing attention for their promising applications in fields of rapid detection due to various merits, such as good fluorescence (FL) stability, favorable bio-compatibility, wide excitation range, superior water dispersibility, and low cost [7–9]. The hydrothermal method is widely used for preparing CQDs due to its advantages of reaction simplicity, low energy consumption, and eco-friendly applications [10,11]. Recently, researchers have extensively studied the hydrothermal method for CQDs preparation using biomass materials, such as orange juice, watermelon juice, and lemon juice as precursors [12]. Among them, food processing waste, including peels [13], roots [14], and kernels [15], have aroused great interest as input materials for preparing CQDs due to their economic and environmental advantages. Nevertheless, the relatively low quantum yields (QY) of CQDs prepared using hydrothermal method have limited their application [16]. Doping of hetero atoms may substantially improve this problem [17]. To this end, nitrogen atoms have been effectively applied to prepare doped-CQDs (N-CQDs), which demonstrated good solubility, sensitive selectivity, excellent stability, and good bio-compatibility with high QY [18–20].

The lanthanides have a wide range of applications in analytical chemistry, biomedicine, and functional materials due to their unique physical, chemical, and optical properties such as a narrow emission band, large Stokes shift, long FL lifetime, and high luminescence intensity [21,22]. Common lanthanide elements are europium (Eu3+) [23,24], terbium (Tb3+) [25,26], lanthanum [27], etc. Among them, Eu3+ has excellent red FL emission characteristics accompanied by nanomaterials when detecting target substances. Complexes formed using Eu3+ and CQDs as FL probes have gained attention for use in rapid detection methods [24]. For example, Abdel-Lateef et al. developed Eu3+-doped carbon dots with unique FL properties to selectively detect metanil yellow dye in turmeric [28]. Wu et al. designed a nanofluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and Eu3+ to selectively detect OTC in water and milk samples [29]. Notably, the complexes formed by Eu3+ and ancillary ligands can exhibit distinct color changes and facilitate convenient visual detection. Jia et al. designed a mobile phone-assisted visual FL platform using CQDs and Eu3+ to provide a real-time detection strategy for the selective detection of tetracycline (TC) in pure milk [30]. Additionally, Yang et al. developed a dual-emitting ratiometric FL sensor for TC, OTC, and doxycycline (DOX) based on boron N-CQDs and Eu3+ [31]. In addition, Tb3+ is also a rare earth element that is widely used in the detection of biological environments [32,33]. For example, Tb3+ could be applied as a kind of sensitive luminescence method for detecting deferiprone in urine and serum and the noninvasive bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells [34,35].

Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [36], high-performance liquid chromatography [37], capillary electrophoresis [38], electrochemical [39], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [40], and immunochemical technology [41] can be used for OTC with high specificity and high sensitivity detection. However, owing to the disadvantages of expensive instruments, cumbersome sample preparation procedures, high requirements, and high costs, it cannot available for point-of-care testing [42]. Fluorometry is considered a more efficient approach owing to its simplicity and convenient operation, making it a better choice for OTC monitoring; however, most single-emission probes are prone to disturbance from detection background and environmental factors. Adopting multiple signals is a potent solution to improve signal stability and achieve accurate and sensitive detection; thus, the ratiometric FL method has been widely explored. Furthermore, ratiometric sensors exhibit marked FL color gradients that are easily analyzed, making them more convenient for visualization than a single-color readout, and provide insights for establishing this research method.

In this study, we developed an N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC detection, providing a tool for rapid visual detection (Scheme 1 ). We utilized smartphones as a platform for real-time, fast, and visual sensing of OTC, offering a promising approach for qualitative identification and semi-quantitative analysis of OTC. We validated the utility of the ratiometric FL sensor by examining pork samples and performing point-of-care testing using the smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor, which holds a broad application prospect in food safety and environmental monitoring.

Bananas were purchased from the local market in Baoding, China. Banana peels were collected and processed into powder through lyophilization in FD-304 (Shandong, China). Diethylenetriamine (DETA) was purchased from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). OTC, TC, DOX, chlortetracycline (CTC), florfenicol (FF), penicillin (PNC), streptomycin (SM), ampicillin (AMP), sulfadiazine (SDI), azithromycin (AZI), enrofloxacin (ENR), cephalexin (CEF), alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), fructose (Fru), lactose (Lac), and cellulose ester dialysis membranes (500 MWCO) were purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tyrosine (Tyr) and lysine (Lys) were purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, sucrose (Suc), ascorbic acid (AA), and glucose (Glu) were obtained from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Waters Oasis HLB cartridges were obtained from Waters Co., Ltd. (Massachusetts, United States). Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solutions ranging from pH 5.0 to 12.0 were used. All other chemicals used were of analytical purity grade, and deionized water was utilized throughout the experiments.

The N-CQDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with freeze-dried banana peel and DETA, following our previously developed procedure with slight modifications [43]. Briefly, 0.85 g of banana peel powder, 300 μL of DETA, and 20 mL of deionized water were sequentially added to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The reaction mixture was then subjected to 10 min ultrasonication to ensure thorough mixing. Subsequently, the mixture was heated at 190 °C for 12 h. The Teflon-lined autoclave was naturally cooled, following which the supernatant was collected with a 5 mL centrifuge tube. The collection solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the resulting brown-yellow product was filtered using a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The purified solution was washed with dichloromethane and dialyzed against a dialysis membrane (500 MWCO) for 48 h to remove any excess reactive material. Optimal conditions for obtaining higher FL intensity were determined through the optimization of banana peel powder dosage (0.55, 0.70, 0.85, 1.00, and 1.15 g), the DETA volume (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600 μL), reaction time (6, 8, 10, 12, and 14 h), and reaction temperature (150, 160, 170, 180, 190, and 200 °C). Taking quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, Φst = 54%, ηst = 1.33) as the standard sample [31], the following formula was used to calculate the QY of the N-CQDs:
(1) ΦN-CQDs = Φst(YN-CQDs/Yst)(Ast/AN-CQDs)(ηst/ηN-CQDs)2
where Φ, Y, A, and η represent the QY, integrated photoluminescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index, respectively. Finally, the purified N-CQDs solution was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for later use.

Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) measurements were conducted using a UV-2802H UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Uneco, China). The FL spectra of N-CQDs were obtained using a Gangdong F-320 FL spectrophotometer (Gangdong, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed using a Tecnai G2 F30 microscope with an acceleration voltage of 300 kV (Oregon, United States). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu IRAFFinity-IS spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Massachusetts, United States).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and optimization of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC
ce:section.ce:para:
The ratiometric FL probe (N-CQDs/Eu3+) for OTC detection was assembled as follows: in a test tube, 50 μL of diluted N-CQDs solution (300-fold) and 100 μL of Eu(NO3)3 solution (15 mg mL−1) were added. Subsequently, 1 mL of OTC solution (0.01 μg mL−1-25 μg mL−1) and 400 μL BR buffer (pH = 10) were added. The mixture solution was supplemented to 3 mL with deionized water. The reaction system was incubated at room temperature for 10 min. The intensity ratio of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor at 621 nm and 445 nm (F621/F445) emission wavelengths was measured at 380 nm excitation wavelength to quantitatively analyze OTC. The limit-of-detection (LOD) of N-CQDs/Eu3+ for OTC detection was calculated using the formula:
LOD =
3
S B
S
, where SB represents the standard error for 10 consecutive scans of the blank sample, and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The experimental conditions were optimized, including the concentration of Eu3+ (5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg mL−1), volume of Eu3+ (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, and 175 μL), volume of BR buffer (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600 μL), pH (5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12), and reaction time (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min), to determine the optimal experimental conditions for OTC detection.

The selectivity of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor was evaluated by examining interfering substances typically found in pork samples, including antibiotics (TC, DOX, CTC, FF, PNC, SM, AMP, SDI, AZI, ENR, and CEF), representative amino acids (Try, Lys, Cys, Ala, Arg, GSH, AA, Glu, Fru, Lac, and Suc), and some inorganic ions (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Cl−, and CO3 2–). Three replicates were performed to ensure the stability and reliability of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC visualization detection
ce:section.ce:para: The color changes of the established system were semi-quantitatively analyzed using the Color Recognizer APP on a smartphone to detect OTC. OTC at different concentrations and the N-CQDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe solution were added into centrifuge tubes to prepare a total volume of 3 mL. The solution in each centrifuge tube was mixed using a vortex mixer and transferred to 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cuvettes. Next, the ratiometric FL sensing system images for the different OTC concentrations were captured using a smartphone under UV light at 365 nm. The color images were converted using the Color Recognizer APP into digital values corresponding to the red (R) and blue (B) color channels, and the red-blue ratio (R/B ratio) was determined using semi-quantitative calculations. For evaluation of the effect of freeze–thaw cycles and bench-top stability on the smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor, samples were prepared and stored at −20 °C and 23 °C, respectively, before being analyzed [35]. The resulting concentrations were compared with the corresponding additions.

Pork muscle and swine urine were used as real samples to validate the practicability of the sensing platform [43]. An aliquot of 5 g (with an accuracy of 0.01 g) of pork muscle and varying volumes of Na2EDTA-Mclivainc butter (20 mL, 20 mL, and 10 mL, 0.1 M) were added to a 50 mL polypropylene centrifuge tube, and subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min in ice water. This step was followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min (temperature below 15 °C) for three repetitions. The supernatants were combined, and the volume was adjusted to 50 mL using the Na2EDTA-Mclivainc butter solution. This was followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min (temperature below 15 °C). Finally, the collected supernatant was purified using HLB solid-phase extraction and stored at 4 °C.

Swine urine was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove residue (temperature below 15 °C). Then, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove impurities. Different concentrations of OTC standard solution were added to each sample, and the FL emission spectrum was measured. All FL measurements were performed three times in parallel.

Banana peel is an environmentally friendly, readily available, and economical biowaste abundant in carbon, making it suitable for CQDs synthesis. In this study, N-CQDs were synthesized using banana peel as a carbon source and DETA as a nitrogen source using a one-step hydrothermal method [44,45]. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the synthesis conditions were optimized by evaluating the FL intensity. The dosage of banana peel powder was optimized within the range of 0.55 to 1.15 g, and the highest FL intensity (666.276 a.u.) was observed at 0.85 g (Fig. S1a). Similarly, a volume of 300 μL of DETA was selected, resulting in a maximum FL intensity of 674.945 a.u. (Fig. S1b). The reaction time and temperature substantially affected the degree of carbonization. Within the tested time range of 6 h to 14 h, the FL intensity of N-CQDs peaked at 12 h (887.220 a.u.) (Fig.S1c). The reaction temperature of 190 °C was chosen as the optimum, yielding an FL intensity of 2052.266 a.u. (Fig. S1d). The FL intensity of N-CQDs gradually increased with increasing reaction temperature, consistent with a previous study in which higher temperature positively affected the carbonization and surface functionalization of N-CQDs [46]. Based on these results, the following optimal parameters were selected: 0.85 g banana peel powder, 300 μL DETA, 12 h reaction time, temperature of 190 °C.

The optical characteristics of N-CQDs were illustrated using UV–Vis absorption and FL spectra, as presented in Fig. 1 a and b. First, the diluted N-CQDs solution appeared as a colorless liquid under natural light and exhibited a bright blue color under a UV lamp (365 nm). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed an absorption peak at 336 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of C = O groups [47]. The FL spectra of N-CQDs exhibited a slight red shift as the excitation wavelength varied from 340 to 390 nm, with an intense luminescence observed at 445 nm when excited at 370 nm. Previous research has reported that various complicating factors, including aspects like size, surface defects, and surface passivation of CQDs, can significantly influence the electronic states and result in the red-shift phenomenon of the FL spectrum [48,49]. In contrast, our study suggests that the observed slight red-shift phenomenon may be primarily attributed to a higher degree of surface passivation and less surface defects of CQDs. This finding is consistent with the work of Chawre, who prepared orange and red CQDs using p-phenylenediamine and tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, respectively [50]. The QY of N-CQDs was measured to be 11.11%. Fan [51] and cooperator were also developed using Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, citric acid, and N, N '-diethylthiourea to synthesize europium-doped carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal method to detect TCs. However, the raw material used in our procedure was more economical and environmentally friendly than that used by them and possessed a high QY. In addition, to study the FL stability of N-CQDs, we detected the change of FL intensity after storage at room temperature for 30 days and under UV irradiation for 90 min. The results are shown in Fig. S2, indicating that the synthesized N-CQDs were significantly stable.

The particle morphology and size distribution of the as-synthesized N-CQDs were examined using high-resolution TEM. The images revealed that the N-CQDs had a quasi-spherical shape and were well-dispersed, displaying uniform dimensions. An inset image in Fig. 1c displays the ordered lattice fringes of the N-CQDs with a spacing of 0.25 nm, which indicates excellent crystallization (Fig. 1c). These findings were consistent with the particle morphology of N-CQDs synthesized using watermelon juice as the precursor by Lu et al. [52]. Moreover, the particle diameters of the N-CQDs were calculated; the average particle diameter was 3.02 nm, ranging from 1.15 to 5.93 nm (Fig. 1d).

The FTIR spectrum was utilized to characterize the surface functional groups of the N-CQDs; the results are presented in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited a broad peak centered at 3040 cm−1, attributed to O-H/N-H and C-H groups' stretching vibrations [53]. The characteristic peaks observed at 2937 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of methyl or methylene (C-H) groups [54]. The peak at 1632 cm−1 could be attributed to CC absorption and –COOH stretching vibrations [55]. The peaks at 1348 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C-N, confirming successful nitrogen doping in N-CQDs [56]. Additionally, the peaks at 1028 cm−1 and 620 cm−1 were attributed to C-O and N-H functional groups on N-CQDs, respectively [57]. The surface of N-CQDs exhibited unsaturated carbonaceous structures, oxygenated groups, and nitrogen groups, enhancing their hydrophilicity.

Furthermore, XPS spectroscopy was used to analyze the elemental compositions and chemical bonds of N-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The full-range XPS spectrum exhibited three peaks at 284.84, 399.55, and 531.24 eV, corresponding to carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s), respectively (Fig. 3a). The mass fraction ratios of C, N, and O were calculated as 78.09%, 14.47%, and 7.44%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3b) could be fitted into four peaks located at 284.77, 285.44, 286.29, and 287.93 eV, linked to C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 399.34, 400.27, and 401.49 eV, corresponding to CN–C, N–(C)3, and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. 3c). Two peaks centered at 531.13 eV and 533.58 eV in the O1s spectrum were attributed to O–C and OC bonds (Fig. 3d). These results indicate the presence of graphitized carbon nuclei and the successful enrichment of hydrophilic groups on the N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and optimization of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the observation that the FL intensity of N-CQDs was quenched and the FL intensity of Eu3+ was significantly increased upon the addition of OTC, a ratiometric FL sensor based on N-CQDs/Eu3+ was designed for OTC detection. The optimal excitation wavelength of N-CQDs was 370 nm and that of Eu3+ was 400 nm (Fig. S3). Considering these excitation wavelengths, 380 nm was selected as the optimum excitation wavelength for the ratiometric FL probe. The intensity ratio of F621/F445 was calculated for optimal performance during OTC detection. Several important parameters of the ratiometric FL probe were investigated, including the concentration of N-CQDs, the amount and volume of Eu3+, the buffer composition, and the reaction time. As illustrated in Fig. S4a, the optimal condition was obtained when the N-CQDs were diluted 30-fold. As illustrated in Fig. S4b and c, the optimal amount and volume of Eu3+ were 15 mg mL−1 and 100 μL, respectively. The optimal pH of the BR buffer was found to be 10 (Fig. S4d), possibly owing to the surface protonation process of N-CQDs under alkaline conditions. This process enhances the coordination capacity of the loop structure, thereby increasing the generation of Eu3+ red emission [58]. Moving forward, the F621/F445 ratio exhibited its highest value with an optimal volume of 400 μL for the BR buffer (Fig. S4e). Fig. S4f illustrated that the response time did not substantially influence the F621/F445 ratio; therefore, a 10-min response time was selected for OTC detection. In general, N-CQDs diluted 30-fold (50 μL), 100 μL Eu3+ (15 mg mL−1), 400 μL BR buffer (pH = 10), and a 10 min response time were chosen for this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimal conditions, a ratiometric FL sensor based on N-CQDs/Eu3+ was developed for OTC detection. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the F621/F445 ratio gradually increased with increasing OTC concentration. The results revealed two distinct linear relationships with OTC concentrations from 0.01 to 5 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9963) and from 5 to 25 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9979) (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the LOD was calculated to be 4.9 ng mL−1 (3σ). The LOD was lower than the permissible OTC concentration (15 μg kg−1) for immunoassay detection in pork muscle, as stated in Bulletin No. 1025-20-2008 from the Ministry of Agriculture, demonstrating that the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor was more sensitive for OTC detection. Compared with the method by He et al., in which a LOD of 0.118 μM was obtained for OTC using a luminescent europium metal–organic framework, the current N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor exhibited improved sensitivity [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the selectivity of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor, the influence of other antibiotics (TC, DOX, CTC, FF, PNC, SM, SDI, AZI, AMP, ENR, and CEF) and a series of possible coexisting substances (Try, Lys, Cys, Ala, Ary, GSH, AA, Glu, Fru, Lac, Suc, K+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Cl−, and CO3 2–) were investigated. As presented in Fig. 5 a and b, obvious signal changes in the F621/F445 ratio were only observed for tetracyclines antibiotics (TC, CTC, and DOX), which have similar chemical structures and functional groups to OTC. The results demonstrated that the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor had excellent selectivity for detecting tetracyclines antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: We performed additional experiments to validate the feasibility of using the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC detection. As presented in Fig. 6 a, the FL intensity of N-CQDs coincided with N-CQDs/Eu3+ (red curve), indicating that the incorporation of Eu3+ had a negligible effect on the FL intensity of N-CQDs. The FL intensity of the Eu3+-OTC compound had a low emission peak (purple curve). In addition, the FL intensity of N-CQDs at 445 nm was quenched, while the FL intensity of Eu3+ at 596 nm and 621 nm markedly increased after introducing OTC (green curve). The emission peak at 621 nm, which had a more distinct response to OTC, was used as the FL signal for Eu3+. Thus, the reverse change of the two FL signals, N-CQDs, and Eu3+, provided a prerequisite for the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor. The response mechanism of the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC testing was evaluated in detail using optimal conditions. To explain the OTC-induced quenching mechanism of N-CQDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of OTC was compared with the excitation spectra of N-CQDs. Partial overlap was present between the excitation spectra of N-CQDs and the absorption spectra of OTC at 354 nm, suggesting that the inner filter effect (IFE) existed between OTC and N-CQDs [60]. Furthermore, the FL lifetime of N-CQDs was almost unchanged from 11.21 to 10.17 ns after introducing OTC (Fig. 6b), revealing that no energy transfer process or dynamic quenching effect was present between N-CQDs and OTC [61]. This result was consistent with the results of Pan et al., which revealed IFE as the major quenching mechanism [62]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra are illustrated in Fig. 6c. The N-CQDs had no substantial influence on the absorption and optical properties of Eu3+, and the results revealed that N-CQDs did not influence the characteristic FL of Eu3+ [63]. OTC effectively increased the FL at 621 nm owing to the specific chelation between OTC and Eu3+ mediated by the β-diketonate configuration, acting as an “antenna” [29] accompanied by the energy transfer Eu3+ caused by the antenna effect (AE) [64]. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the N-CQDs + Eu3++OTC emission peak exhibited a noticeable red shift and an enhanced absorption intensity compared with OTC, indicating the chelation of OTC with Eu3+ [19]. This was consistent with the result of Chen et al., where the emission peak increased at 596 nm (5D0→7F1) and 621 nm (5D0→7F2) after the addition of OTC to N-CQDs/Eu3+ due to the characteristic transition of Eu3+ [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC visualization detection
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor exhibited successive color changes from blue to purple under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 7 b). This observation was further verified using the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage's (CIE) color coordinate diagram (Fig. 7a) and achieved similar results. Therefore, we proposed OTC detection using a mobile phone color reading and analysis function [66]. A portable, easy, and economical smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor was designed by integrating a smartphone application, Color Recognizer APP, as a signal reader and analyzer to achieve on-site and real-time semi-quantitative OTC detection. The images were obtained using the smartphone camera. Through analysis, the semi-quantitative calculation of the R/B ratio was used to obtain the concentration of OTC in the samples. With increasing OTC concentration, the percentage of R progressively increased while the proportion of B gradually weakened. As presented in Fig. 7b, a linear relationship was observed between the R/B ratio and diverse concentrations of OTC in the range of 0.7–20 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9955). Consequently, we propose using this method based on the N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for convenient field and visual OTC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor for OTC detection in pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: These results indicated that the established method was promising for on-site and real-time quantitative OTC detection in animal-derived foods. In recent years, other sensors have been developed to detect OTC using CQDs as detection elements, which we have compared in Table S1. We found that our innovative method achieved a comparable or better LOD and visual linear range of OTC than other methods. Thus, we have developed an economical and environmentally friendly FL sensor using banana peel waste as raw material, which can reliably detect OTC residues in pork samples.

The N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor was developed as a novel rapid, and selective method for OTC detection. N-CQDs with good water solubility, prominent FL, and favorable stability were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method employing banana peel and DETA as precursors. The complexes formed by N-CQDs and Eu3+ were stable and effectively overcame the interference of external environmental factors. The N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor exhibited a good linear relationship between 0.01 and 5 μg mL−1 and 5 to 25 μg mL−1. The minimum detection limit was 4.9 ng mL−1 OTC, and the sensor could detect OTC within 10 min. This sensing mechanism was based on the synergistic effect of IFE and AE on OTC caused by N-CQDs and Eu3+, respectively. Using the smartphone chromaticity analysis program, we developed a smartphone-assisted ratiometric FL probe of N-CQDs/Eu3+ for visual and real-time OTC detection through FL color changes. OTC in pork muscle and swine urine samples were detected. The developed N-CQDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor and the smartphone-assisted detection platform have the potential for broader applications for utilizing biological waste to detect antibiotics in animal foods safety monitoring. The OTC detection strategy introduced in this study warrants further enhancements in terms of specificity and sensitivity of the reaction system. Additionally, the structural similarities among TCs present a challenge in specifically detecting OTC, which will guide our future research endeavors. Thus, we intend to incorporate aptamers to address these issues, which necessitates further research and exploration.

Chen Wu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Tong Zhou: Supervision, Methodology. Zhe Gao: Software, Investigation. Meijiao Li: Supervision, Validation. Qian Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Wen Zhao: Formal analysis.
